title: practical english usage - Part 287
content:
They  can be used to correct mistakes or misunderstandings.

He's twelve, but he actually still believes in Father Christmas.

’ How did you get on with my car? ’ Well, actually, I'm terribly sorry, I'm afraid She was so angry that she actually tore up the letter.

only 20 miles.

It takes me an hour to drive to work, although the actual distance is Actual and actually often introduce surprising or unexpected information.

’ Did you enjoy your trip? ’ Very much,  actually.' I've got a new job. Actually, they've made me sales manager.

It's over 100 kilos. Let me look. Yes, the actual weight is 108 kilos.

They  are used to make things clearer, more precise or more definite.

Actual means ‘ real’ ; actually means 'really’  or 'in fact’ .

1  meaning       and   use 358  actual      (ly)   _____________                   ____ For other uses of these words, see a good dictionary.

For over and above, ►    354.   For across from (AmE), ►    549.1.

We drove through several towns.

- We drove across the desert.

I walked through the wood. (I was in the wood.) - We walked across the ice. (We were on the ice.) dimensional space, with things on all sides. Compare: on and in. Through, unlike across, is used for a movement in a three- The  difference between across and through is like the difference between 5  across       and   through I'm going over to Jack's.  Shall we drive over and see your mother?

We   often use over (to) for short journeys.

Note that the adverb over has a wider meaning than the preposition over.

4 the   adverb      over     (to) It took them six hours to row across the lake.

He walked right across the desert.

We   usually prefer across to say 'on/to the other side of a flat area or surface’ .

3 flat   areas:   across       preferred not Why are you climbing over the wall? (     .. .across the wall?) We   prefer over to say 'on/to the other side of something high’ .

2 high    things:    over     preferred vocabulary •  360   after, adverb not (     ... and after, I'm going...) I'm going to do my exams, and afterwards I'm going to study medicine.

like afterwards (AmE also afterward), then or after that.

After is not normally used alone as an adverb. Instead, we  use other expressions 2  after      not   used    alone They started the job on the 16th and finished three weeks later.

In more exact expressions of time, later is more common.

We had oysters for supper. Shortly after, I began to feel ill.

a few days after, etc.

After can be used in adverb phrases like shortly after, long after, 1  shortly        after,      etc 360 after:     adverb                    ______ For information about -ing forms and infinitives after ajraid, ►     105.13.

Jack's afraid. Jack's a frightened man. (     ... an afraid man.) 'attributive position' (►   183). Compare: Afraid is one of the adjectives that are not usually used before a noun in 3 not   used    before     a  noun 'It's going to rain.' 'Yes, I'm afraid so.' 'Can you lend me a pound? 'I'm afraid not.' I'm afraid so/not are used as short answers.

I'm afraid (that) there's been an accident.

I'm afraid (that) I can't help you.

apologetic refusals and bad news.

I'm afraid (that) often means I'm sorry to tell you (that). It is used to introduce 2  I'm  afraid       = 'I'm sorry' Are you afraid of the dark?   I'm not afraid to say what I think.

Don't be afraid, (     Don't fear)  She's afraid that I might find out.

In an informal style, be afraid is more common than fear.

1 afraid       and   fear 359 afraid For more about actually and similar discourse markers. ►    284.3, 301.3.

(     .. . than it is actually.) In 1840 the population of Ireland was higher than it is now.

What's our current financial position? (     .. .-our-actual financial position?) moment, now, at present.

attual(ment)e. We   express these ideas with present, current, up to date; at this They  do not mean the same as, for example, aetuel(lement), aktuell, or Actual and actually are 'false friends' for people who  speak some languages.

2 'false    friends' Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 361 vocabulary •          after all beforethree years.) not I met that woman in Scotland three years ago. (     ... three-years l>efmtr/ from a past moment. Compare: We   can use before in the same way (with a past perfect tense) to count back present; to say how  long before now something happened.
